mcqs.question: What does it mean for a problem to be in the complexity class NP?

options.text: It can be solved by a non-deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.
It can be solved by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.
A proposed solution to it can be verified by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.
It cannot be solved in polynomial time.

mcqs.explanation: The defining characteristic of NP is the ability to verify a given certificate or solution quickly (in polynomial time), not necessarily to find the solution quickly. Option A is a common misconception about what NP stands for, though it is a valid alternative definition. Option B defines class P. Option D defines problems outside of P.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is a necessary and sufficient condition for a problem C to be NP-Complete?

options.text: C is in NP and C is NP-Hard.
C is not in P.
C is in NP and C is polynomial-time reducible to SAT.
C is NP-Hard.

mcqs.explanation: NP-Complete problems are the hardest problems in NP. This requires them to be both in NP (verifiable) and NP-Hard (as hard as every problem in NP). Being just NP-Hard (Option D) is not sufficient, as an NP-Hard problem might not be in NP. Option C describes a common technique for proving NP-Hardness but is not the definition.

mcqs.question: If a polynomial-time algorithm is discovered for a single NP-Complete problem, what is the immediate consequence?

options.text: All NP problems become solvable in polynomial time.
All NP-Hard problems become solvable in polynomial time.
P becomes equal to NP.
The problem was never truly NP-Complete to begin with.

mcqs.explanation: Since NP-Complete problems are the hardest in NP, if one of them is in P, it implies that every problem in NP is also in P (because any NP problem can be reduced to the NP-Complete problem, which is now solvable in poly-time). This would prove P = NP. It would not necessarily mean all NP-Hard problems are in P, as some NP-Hard problems may not be in NP.

mcqs.question: What is the significance of the Cook-Levin theorem?

options.text: It proved that P ≠ NP.
It proved that Boolean Satisfiability (SAT) is in P.
It provided the first example of an NP-Complete problem.
It proved that all problems in NP are also in P.

mcqs.explanation: The Cook-Levin theorem was foundational. It demonstrated that the Boolean Satisfiability (SAT) problem is NP-Complete. This provided a crucial first link in the chain of reductions, allowing us to prove other problems are NP-Complete by reducing SAT to them. It did not resolve P vs NP (Options A and D), and SAT is not known to be in P (Option B).

mcqs.question: Which technique is primarily used to prove that a new problem X is NP-Complete?

options.text: Showing that X can be solved by a non-deterministic Turing machine.
Providing a polynomial-time reduction from a known NP-Complete problem to X.
Providing a polynomial-time reduction from X to a known NP-Complete problem.
Showing that no polynomial-time algorithm exists for X.

mcqs.explanation: The standard proof technique involves: 1) showing X is in NP, and 2) showing X is NP-Hard. NP-Hardness is proven by reducing a known NP-Complete problem Y to X (Y ≤ₚ X). This shows that solving X is at least as hard as solving Y. Option C (X ≤ₚ Y) would only show that X is no harder than Y, which is not useful for a hardness proof.
